- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.

- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

- observable feature: feature value

</kb>

referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- object shape: elongated body with wings extending from the sides
- object position: appears to be in the sky
- object color: shades of blue and green
- presence of propeller: visible at the front of the object
</features>
<kb>
- IF [object shape = elongated body with wings AND object position = in the sky OR object position = on the ground] THEN [0]
- IF [object shape = squat, rounded body with visible limbs AND object position = on the ground or partially submerged] THEN [6]
- IF [object shape = four-legged animal with a mane and tail AND object position = on the ground] THEN [7]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 0 fires because the query image shows an object with an elongated body and wings, and it is positioned in the sky, which matches the observable features for class 0.
</rule_check>
<response>0</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

<features>
- object shape: elongated body with wings extending from the sides
- object position: appears to be in the sky
- object color: shades of blue and green
- presence of propeller: visible at the front of the object
</features>
<kb>
- IF [object shape = elongated body with wings AND object position = in the sky OR object position = on the ground] THEN [0]

<response>0</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- overall shape: somewhat rounded, irregular blob
- color: predominantly light brown/tan with some darker spots/texture
- texture: mottled, bumpy appearance
- limbs: short, thick limbs visible, suggesting a squat posture
- background: appears to be a textured, earthy surface
</features>
<kb>
- IF [overall shape is elongated with distinct wings and/or tail, or a clear outline of an aircraft] THEN [0]
- IF [overall shape is a squat, somewhat rounded or irregular blob, with mottled or bumpy texture, and short, thick limbs visible] THEN [6]
- IF [overall shape is a large, four-legged animal with a distinct head, body, and tail, often with a mane or bridle visible] THEN [7]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 6 fires because the Query Image shows an overall squat, somewhat rounded, irregular blob shape, with a mottled/bumpy texture, and short, thick limbs visible, which aligns with the observable features of class 6 examples.
</rule_check>
<response>6</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- main subject: animal with a rider
- animal shape: quadrupedal, horse-like
- background: green/brown, possibly foliage
- foreground: brown, possibly ground
- additional objects: red and white striped barrier
</features>
<kb>
- IF [main subject is an airplane or aircraft] THEN [0]
- IF [main subject is a frog or toad] THEN [6]
- IF [main subject is a horse, with or without a rider] THEN [7]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule "IF [main subject is a horse, with or without a rider] THEN [7]" fires because the query image clearly shows a horse with a rider as the main subject.
</rule_check>
<response>7</response>
